titleStmt.title: SII 25.142A: original edition by M. D. Sampath
No. 142A. (A.R. No. 142A of 1908.) KUḶḶAPUṞAM, PERIYAKULAM TALUK, MADURAI DISTRICT.

summary.p: West wall of the central shrine in the Sundararājaperumāḷ temple.
On palaeographical grounds, this fragmentary record may be assigned to 13-14th Century A.D.

It records an order of the king issued to the sabhai of Rājachūḍāmaṇich-chaturvēdimaṅgalam granting 4 mā of lands measured by kaḍamai-iṟukkuṅ-kōl at Kaṇṇappaḷḷam in the above village as nandavaṉappuṟa-iṟaiyili, for the flower-garden raised by Tiruvuḍaiyāḷ, one of the queens (nam peṇḍugaḷ) of the king (whose name is lost), to god Aḻagiyamaṇavāḷapperumāḷ. It is said that a Śrīvaishṇava of this temple Niṉṟa-Nambi alias Tirumāliruñjōlai-nambi of Karāmbiṟchēṭṭu and his descendants were to rear the flower-garden from the land grant received.

The name of the queen and the signatory Tirumalaiyuḍaiyāṉ alias Chēdirājaṉ, who is referred to as the mantri (minister) of Perumaṇalūr in Aṇḍa-nāḍu are identical with their namesake figuring in the 5th year record of Māṟavarmaṉ Sundara-pāṇḍya ‘who took the Chōḷa Country’. (cf: A.R.Ep., 1908, No. 148). Hence, our record may be assigned to the same Māṟavarmaṉ Sundar-pāṇḍya i.e., Māṟaº Sundara-pāṇḍya I.

The record No. 142 A, refers to Tiruvuḍaiyāḷ as nam-peṇḍugaḷ. This Tiruvuḍaiyāḷ has also figured in a record of the 5th year of Māṟavarman Sundara-pāṇḍya, who took the Chōḷa country from the same place i.e., Kuḷḷappuṟam. (No. 148 of 1908). She is referred to therein as nam-peṇḍugaḷ only. But in a record of the second year of a king (whose name is lost), she is designated as māmatti. In the absence of any confirmatory evidence except the praśasti, Pūmalar-tiruvum, etc., it is not possible to say whether she is ‘the wife of maternal uncle’ of the king, and if so, it is not clear why she should figure in a record of Māṟavarmaṉ Sundarapāṇḍya (I) as nam-peṇḍugaḷ, unless it is interpreted as meaning ‘wife’ of the king in question. In which case, it indicates that Māṟavarmaṉ Sundara-pāṇḍya I was the maternal uncle of Māṟavarmaṉ Sundara-pāṇḍya II, which is hitherto unknown. From this record, it may be inferred that Tiruvuḍaiyār was the wife (queen) of Māṟavarmaṉ Sundara-pāṇḍya I. Her name continued to occur in the reign of his successor Māṟaº Sundara-pāṇḍya II. The praśasti Pūmalar-tiruvum, etc., found in the inscriptions from Aḻagarkōyil in Melur Taluk of Madurai District (Nos. 9, 17 and 18 of 1931-32) and Tirumālugandāṉkōṭṭai in Mudukulattur Taluk of Ramanathapuram District (Nos. 36, 38 and 54 of 1931-32) has been attributed to Māṟavarmaṉ Sundara-pāṇḍya II (See A.R.Ep., 1931-32, part II, para 22).

revisionDesc.change: Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

div.p:
The beginnings and ends of the lines are lost. —ḷic ceyal . Irājacūṭāmaṇic catu[ rvveti maṅka] lattu sabhaiyāṟku . Aḻakiya maṇavāḷap perumāḷukku nam peṇṭukaḷil tiruvuṭaiyāḷ ceyvikkiṟa tirunanta vaṉattukkut taṅkaḷurk kuṟuvai peru—
—yokam Uṭpaṭa nantavaṉappuṟa Iṟaiyiliyāka Iṭappeṟa veṇumeṉṟu Ivaḷ namakkuc coṉṉamaiyil taṅkaḷurk kaṇṇaṉppaḷḷattile kaṭamaiyiṟukkuṅkolāl nālu mānilam—
—viṉiyokaṅ koṇṭu Ikkoyiṟ tiruppati śrī vaiṣṇavaril karāmpiṟ ceṭṭut tirumāliruñcolai nampi makaṉ niṉṟa nampiyāṉa tirumāḷikai ceytāṉ dāsaṉum Ivaṉ vaṟkattārum tirunantavaṉañ cey—
—paṭi cantirātittavaṟ celvatāka kalliluñ cempilum veṭṭuvittuk kuṭukka . Ivai Aṇṭa nāṭṭup perumaṇalūr mantiri tirumalaiyuṭaiyāṉāṉa cetirājaṉ Eḻuttu Ivai kiḻ cempināṭṭu pālaṅ—The rest of the record is lost.
Digital edition of SII 25.142A by automatically converted to DHARMA conventions.
